data.title: Bank Of Korea Easing May Be Over Amid Improved Growth Outlook
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang, Senior Economist, South Korea and Japan

Today’s meeting sent strong signals the BoK’s rate-cutting cycle has come to an end

The odds of further Bank of Korea easing seem low amid upgraded forecasts for South Korea’s GDP and inflation. Increased inflation concerns, risks linked to the weak KRW, and ongoing financial instability were all reflected in changes to the BoK’s forward guidance in the policy meeting statement.

In its forward guidance, the BoK removed the phrase “maintain rate cut stance” and instead added that it is “leaving room for potential rate cuts.” In our view, the BoK intends to convey that its easing cycle remains ongoing rather than concluded to mitigate the risk of abrupt movements in financial markets. Also, they appear to be closely monitoring any sudden change in market rates that may signal an impending rate hike.

Governor Rhee stated that it’s premature to consider a rate hike at this time, emphasising that changes in policy direction generally require a 12-month implementation period.

Additionally, today's decision was not unanimous. There was one dissenting vote, the same as the last meeting. Opinions on a rate cut in the next three months remain divided, and support for easing has dropped from four to three. Yet the idea has not been abandoned.

Nevertheless, it is anticipated that changes in macroeconomic conditions will prompt the BoK to end its easing stance.

The BoK have revised up its GDP and CPI outlook

The BoK expects the economy to grow 1.8% year on year (vs 1.6% previously) in 2026 and 1.9% in 2027. Strong chips and the IT cycle will boost growth more than previously expected. Stronger-than-expected third-quarter GDP is the main reason for the upward revision of 2025 GDP outlook to 1.0% (vs 0.9% previously).

In our view, the revision was rather moderate. We expect GDP to rise by 1.2% in 2025 and 2.0% in 2026 amid stronger-than-expected private consumption and exports. If upcoming data is solid, in line with our expectations, the BoK will revise up its outlook again next year.

CPI inflation for 2026 has been revised up from 1.9% to 2.1%, likely due to recent KRW weakness, which has driven higher-than-expected inflation. A 2.1% rate inflation isn’t a big deviation from the BoK’s target level, and thus shouldn’t be a major concern.

GDP/CPI outlook : ING vs BoK

Concerns over household credit and high housing prices continued

Strict government controls on mortgages and home buying are gradually slowing the housing market, but underlying demand remains strong. The recent consumer sentiment survey showed that expectations for housing prices dropped but remain elevated. Thus, the BoK will have to monitor the housing market trends. We anticipate that the housing market will stabilise, at least in the short term, as the government considers implementing stricter measures. Nevertheless, this is unlikely to provide sufficient confidence for the BoK to lower the policy rate.

The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: Janus Henderson Global Research Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment environment

  • Global equities rose strongly in U.S. dollar terms on easing trade tensions and signs of resilient economic growth in many countries.
  • U.S. market performance was especially strong, aided by expectations for Federal Reserve (Fed) interest rate cuts. The Fed reduced policy rates in September, with additional rate cuts expected by year-end.
  • The market advance was relatively narrow, with notable gains in information technology stocks that rose on excitement around artificial intelligence (AI). The more defensive consumer staples sector detracted from relative performance.

Portfolio review

Relative performance was hindered by an investment in Intuit (INTU). The company’s enterprise software products include Quickbooks, an accounting, payroll, and payments solution for small- and mid-sized businesses, and TurboTax, a leading tax preparation software. The stock rallied earlier in the year on strong results for the tax software business, particularly for TurboTax Live, which combines tax software, human experts, and AI capabilities. Intuit also reported strong results for its mid-market Quickbooks business. Following this performance, shares of Intuit sold off in the third quarter on investor concerns about its guidance for fiscal year 2026. This weaker guidance reflected headwinds for Intuit’s Mailchimp business, rather than for its core Quickbooks business. Investors have also been concerned about how generative AI may impact software businesses that use Google Search to drive spending. We believe the stock sell-off was overdone, and we remain invested as we are encouraged by the recent performance of TurboTax and Quickbooks.

Progressive (PGR) was also a relative detractor. The insurer has been a strong performer in recent years, as robust policy growth and improving underwriting profitability in the personal automobile insurance market supported earnings expansion. The stock lagged in the third quarter, however, due to investor concerns about the sustainability of its strong policy growth and pricing power. Insurance stocks, more broadly, have also detracted from relative results in a market focused on AI opportunities and pro-cyclical financials. On a positive note, Progressive’s underwriting profitability remained strong. Growth has also persisted, though at a slower pace relative to recent years. We believe Progressive remains well positioned to gain market share due to its competitive advantages in scale, risk segmentation, and distribution.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) (TSM) was a relative contributor. As one of the world's dominant chip manufacturers, TSMC is the leading supplier of graphic processing units to companies, such as NVIDIA, that are at the forefront of the AI buildout. Excitement around the AI supply chain grew as leading hyperscalers and other technology companies continued to announce substantive investments in high performance computing. We believe TSMC is well positioned to capitalize on this spending due to its unparalleled scale, deep technological advantages, and its partnerships with major semiconductor companies and hyperscalers. The company has further expanded its market leadership as competitors have stumbled or shifted away from the foundry business, leaving TSMC as the unparallel market leader in this space.

Lam Research (LRCX), another contributor, manufactures specialized deposition and etching tools critical to producing the advanced chips needed for AI. Lam has become a structural share gainer in wafer fabrication equipment (WFE) because of both its strong customer relationships and rising etch and deposition intensity across leading-edge foundry/logic, DRAM, and advanced packaging. Shares of LAM rose as the company exceeded its revenue and earnings target, driven by strength across business lines and market share gains in the WFE market. Operational improvements also supported margin improvement. We see long-term opportunity for Lam Research as a market leader in many of the technologies that enable AI.

Manager outlook

The market has continued to rally on strong corporate earnings news and relatively stable U.S. economic growth. Investors have also moved away from their worst-case fears about U.S. trade policy, which has boosted the performance of risk assets. The Fed has started to lower policy rates, which may help reduce funding costs while providing a renewed tailwind for some market sectors, such as residential construction. Additionally, we have been encouraged by positive economic news outside of the U.S., especially in Europe, which may offer opportunities for companies with international exposure.

As we continue to monitor shifting macro and policy currents and their impact on the companies, we remain committed to our bottom-up, fundamentally driven investment process. The foundation of this process is our exceptional team of analysts, who bring deep knowledge of their respective sectors. Our analyst team is constantly on the lookout for companies with strong or improving business models, exceptional management teams, and underappreciated competitive advantages. Through our time-tested, disciplined investment approach, we will continue to pursue our goal of long-term growth in capital.

data.title: Coca-Cola: Strong Quarter, Stronger Margins - But Limited Upside
data.description:
The reading I made about Coca-Cola (KO) in my previous article is reinforced after analyzing in detail the results of Q3 2025. At this point, KO gives me the feeling of being a company operating almost on autopilot, but in the best sense, since it operates the business with millimeter precision even when the global context is very heterogeneous and when consumption dynamics vary region by region.

What strikes me most in reviewing this quarter is not the growth itself, which was solid, but the consistency. KO grew back in line with the high of its structural goal, defended margins even with more marketing investment, sustained volume, and continued to gain global share. In short, I confirmed what I already sensed, and that is that the competitive advantage of the company not only remains intact but seems to be strengthening.

However, as I pointed out in September, the problem lies not in execution but in the valuation that the market validates by this predictability. Today, KO continues to trade with multiples above its historical averages and a considerable premium against the sector. So, although I value the stability it offers, I see little room for expansion of multiples at these prices. In this scenario, I maintain my hold posture.

Q3 2025: Fine Execution, Solid Organic Growth, and Surprising Margins Again

According to Q3 results, net operating revenues grew 5% YoY to $12.5B, and organic revenue rose 6% YoY, driven by a price mix of 6% and a volume that increased 1%. Since the conference, they mentioned that the quarter started slower, but September brought an acceleration that allowed them to comply with the operational guidance without any problems. When I see this combination (stable or slightly positive volume plus sustained pricing), it is when I perceive the strength of the portfolio.

The management summed it up clearly:

We are confident we can deliver on our 2025 guidance while also working to achieve our longer term objectives.

Earnings confirmed that reading. Comparable EPS was $0.82, up 6% YoY despite a negative FX impact of also 6%, and diluted EPS for the quarter was $0.86. For me, these results show that KO managed to turn a correct quarter into a very solid one precisely because it was able to expand profits in a context that did not play completely in its favor.

Where I most notice the quality of business is on the margins. Comparable gross margin was close to 61%, with an underlying expansion of around 80 bps YoY, while the comparable operating margin was 31.87%, advancing about 115 bps YoY. And the most interesting data, in my opinion, is the expansion of the underlying operating margin (~260 bps), which reflects the combination of organic growth, efficiency, and cost control. It strikes me that this is happening while KO continues to increase its investment in marketing, something that demonstrates operational discipline.

In short, what I see is continuity. Coca-Cola not only grows but also turns that growth into higher profitability quarter after quarter, and that is a clear sign of a company with a mature but not stagnant model.

Performance by Region: A Portfolio That Resists Almost Any Scenario

At this point in the analysis, when I review region by region, what interests me most is not only the number of points but also how KO reacts to each context. And in North America that was very clear. While volumes remained stable, there was strong growth in revenue and profits. What struck me most was the way in which they adjusted the commercial execution, with more equipment placement, better product availability, and very fine work on pricing per channel. These tactical decisions, which are not always seen in the headlines, explain why KO continues to gain share even in developed markets.

In Latin America, however, the results were more varied. Brazil showed a very solid performance, Chile and Colombia remained stable, and Mexico continued to show the macro weakness already expected. Even so, KO regained value share in the region and maintained positive organic revenue at constant currency. For me, this reinforces something that I have been seeing for a long time, and it is that, even with a complicated macro, the system knows how to defend pricing and volume in a surgical way.

On the other hand, Europe and EMEA had a better quarter than the environment suggested. Europe faced demanding bases, but KO still managed to grow revenues and results with campaigns linked to major events and precise management of price architecture. In the Middle East and Africa, volume directly grew despite a volatile macro, which I think is one of the most telling indicators of portfolio strength. When a company performs well in unstable markets, it tends to perform even better in stable ones.

Asia-Pacific had a more mixed dynamic. India, China, and the Philippines were affected by adverse climate, lower consumption, and sectoral weakness, while Japan and Australia showed much more stable performance. This mixture generated a composition effect that artificially inflated the average price, but without changing the real trend... There is growth in this region, yes, but it is not homogeneous.

And when I look at all the information together, undoubtedly what stands out most is consistency, as KO gained global value participation for the 18th consecutive quarter. And, frankly, for me that regularity is worth more than one or two points of additional growth.

Cash Flow and Balance: Behind the Noise, the Same Solidity as Always

When I look back at the cash flow numbers, the first thing I do is separate the extraordinary from what really matters. And in this quarter, that difference is immediately noticeable. The FCF may give a lukewarm impression at first, but once I get the one-time impact of contingent payment by fairlife, my reading is totally different. Until September, the adjusted FCF reached $8.5B, driven by better operational performance and lower taxes, although with a somewhat more moderate contribution from working capital. For me, this metric is the one that best reflects the actual cash generation of the business, and I see it as very solid.

The year-end guidance points to an adjusted FCF of at least $9.8B, a level that I consider more than healthy and fully aligned with what one expects from KO in a normal year.

The balance reinforces this idea. Net debt over EBITDA remains close to 1.8x, below the target range of 2-2.5x, which in practice gives Coca-Cola ample room to continue investing, financing growth in strategic categories such as proteins and premium beverages, and sustaining repurchases and dividends without unnecessary stress.

In other words, even after an extraordinary outlay, the company continues to operate with a comfortable, stable, and predictable capital structure.

Valuation, Growth, and Profitability: The Gap Remains

When I move from operational results to valuation, the feeling KO leaves me with is practically the same as I had in my previous analysis. The business operates with admirable precision, but the market pays so dearly for that stability that it leaves little room for a positive surprise.

While the P/E FWD is at 23.83x, 54% above the sector, the EV/EBITDA FWD stands at 21.10x, more than twice the average of comparable companies. Even P/S trades at a premium of over 400% against the sector. When I see all these multiples together, what I interpret is that the market not only recognizes the quality of KO but overcompensates for it.

Growth, while very solid, does not quite explain that gap. Revenues rose 2.8% YoY, EBITDA grew 11.39% YoY, and diluted EPS increased 25.34%, although on a favorable comparative basis. The estimated growth of EPS at 3-5 years is around 6.56%, a stable and predictable pace, but far from justifying such high multiples.

Profitability is undoubtedly where KO again shows its structural advantage. A gross margin of 61.63%, an EBIT margin of 31.93%, and a net margin of 27.34% are levels that most consumer companies can only look at from afar. And the ROE of 45.12%, along with an ROA of 12.29%, confirms that the company creates value consistently, even in years with macro shocks or complex FX.

But even with that extraordinary profitability, I find it hard to see a strong upside from these multiples. For me, the conclusion is almost the same as in September, and it is that while KO is a company of undisputed quality, the price reflects it completely. The market is paying a premium for sleeping well, and although I understand that, at this level it also limits potential returns for those who enter today.

Risks

At this point, the risks facing Coca-Cola are not a surprise, but they remain important. The first is FX, and as we saw this quarter, a negative impact can wipe out much of earnings. In large markets such as Mexico, this front is even more sensitive.

The second risk is pricing. While I note that KO managed to manage elasticities with great precision, that doesn’t mean it’s automatic. Each adjustment requires calibration by channel, by package, and by economic context. And when the consumer begins to feel pressure, each price point demands more subtlety.

Another risk to consider is regulatory. Every day there are more sugar taxes, changes in packaging, and environmental standards that can generate additional costs or restrictions. While this is not new and Coca-Cola has been handling these issues for decades, it is a structural risk that never goes away.

Still, I point out that the company has two advantages that balance this risk map, and these are global scale and operational discipline. In my experience following KO, these two factors have allowed repeated shocks to be absorbed without affecting the long-term vision.

Conclusion

After reviewing Q3, my feeling about Coca-Cola didn’t change. I see a company that continues to run with precision, defending margins, gaining share, and showing a consistency that few global companies can replicate. Quarter by quarter, the data confirms that the business model works even when the environment does not follow.

But, at the same time, the other side of the story also becomes clear again, and it is that the stock trades at demanding multiples, still above its historical averages and with a marked premium against the sector. So, although KO provides predictability, stable flow, and profitability that stand out in any comparison, the current valuation leaves little room for further expansion.

For defensive and revenue-oriented profiles, KO remains almost a reference, but for those looking for more aggressive upside, the entry still seems to me tight.

For all this, I keep my position in hold. It is an excellent business, but today it is purchased at a price that fully reflects that excellence.

data.title: Oportun Financial: Trading At A Discount To Book Value Amid Macro Uncertainty
data.description:
Oportun Financial (OPRT) posted Q3 results earlier this month. While the stock appears to be down amid fears of the macro situation, the business is in a steady state, at a discount to its tangible book value, and so I've maintained my Buy rating.

Summary of Previous Thesis

When I covered OPRT last time, I noted that it was trading at a discount to tangible book value per share, despite being profitable. This seemed tied to concerns in the wider market about potential impacts of President Trump's tariffs, the then-lack of rate cuts by the Fed in 2025, and how these might impact the nonprime borrowers on Oportun's loan book.

As a reminder, Oportun gives personal loans to nonprime borrowers, which are typically unsecured by any collateral and rely on high APRs (up to 36%) to price in the underwriting risk.

Nevertheless, the book was showing quarterly improvements in net charge-offs and delinquencies. Not only that, but cost-cutting measures were also being successfully implemented. I felt the discount to TBV per share was a reasonable case for undervaluation and rated them a Buy.

Q3 Updates

Oportun delivered another profitable quarter, their fourth consecutive one.

While GAAP income was down slightly from Q2, it reflected a large improvement from the prior-year quarter. The profits aren't just good on their own. The balance sheet as of Q3 shows the improvements made in their capital structure.

This is largely seen in the shift of their secured and asset-backed financing. During the earnings call, management explained:

We executed ABS financings at weighted average yields below 6% in August and October and proactively repaid higher cost corporate debt. Additionally, we expanded our warehouse financing capacity in October by adding a new facility and modifying an existing one, extending average maturity and reducing our average cost of capital.

Above, I've indicated the portions of their ABS borrowings and the changes between the end of 2024 and the end of Q3. The lower-cost portions have been kept intact, while the higher-cost portions were rolled into new ABS issues. Management said of this:

...cost of debt was lower sequentially, decreasing from 8.6% in the second quarter to 8.1% in the third quarter, closely aligning with our 8% unit economics target.

As far as total leverage is concerned, Q3 marked a streak of continued decreases, now at 7.1x debt-to-equity.

While we often look at net interest income and the balance sheet when analyzing lenders, it's also important to note Oportun's improvement in their overhead. Q3's operating expenses were down over $9M (11%) from the prior year, contributing to a $43M reduction year-to-date.

With attention on the underwriting, 30+ day delinquencies did tick up in the quarter, but net charge-offs remained stable.

Overall, it was a quarter that showed stable earnings, a steady loan book, improved capital structure, and more cost-effective overhead.

Outlook and Valuation

Let's revisit my previous metric for valuation: TBV per share, which we'll calculate with fully diluted shares outstanding.

Taking into account potentially dilutive RSUs, there were 48.3M shares outstanding in Q3. With this, common equity of $383.9M in Q3 and capitalized software and other intangibles of $74.8M, TBV per share comes to $6.40. The recent stock price of $4.80 is an attractive discount of 25%, and as long as they remain profitable, TBV per share should grow.

Management offered insights on cautious preparation for a macro environment showing signs of stress, stating in the call:

So we think that right now in this economy, it's important to try to decrease loan size and really focus on making payments affordable. And that's because though we think that the consumer today continues to be very resilient, there are certainly pressure points, right?

They also spoke to increasing the priority of their secured loan product in order to make the overall book lower in risk. I take these as signs that management is bracing for some impact, even though the current book is a "post-tightening" vintage. They understand that things can become harder.

The target debt-to-equity multiple is 6x, so earnings will be retained to improve that or reduce an outstanding debt obligation. As they continue to do this, I expect they can also refinance other portions of their debt at lower costs of capital, increasing EPS and the likelihood that OPRT is at a true discount.

Because of these remarks, I think a risk-managed entry price is in order, and I will readjust an entry price of $5.00 or less for this stock going forward.

Conclusion

Oportun isn't showing signs of trouble, but management is clearly preparing for that possibility, and so the current discount isn't too surprising. Investors can manage the risks by being disciplined on the entry price and the sizing. Because the discount seems sufficient at this point, however, I rate the stock a Buy.

data.title: Columbia International Dividend Income Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market overview

Per the MSCI ACWI ex USA Index, international markets built on their positive returns from the first half of the year, returning 6.89% in the third quarter. Ten of the eleven sectors within the benchmark posted a positive return during the quarter.

The third quarter of 2025 was marked by resilient, but uneven global growth, as economies navigated the lingering effects of elevated tariffs, shifting trade alliances and monetary policy recalibrations. Trade dynamics were a central theme throughout the quarter. Although some bilateral trade agreements — particularly between the U.S. and key partners like Japan and the E.U. — helped reduce uncertainty, the broader landscape was still shaped by protectionist policies. The U.S. implemented new tariffs on strategic imports such as copper, pharmaceuticals and consumer electronics, which contributed to rising input costs and inflationary pressures globally. These measures also prompted retaliatory actions from trading partners, further complicating supply chains and dampening cross-border investment flows. Central banks around the world continued to recalibrate their policy approaches during the third quarter, responding to a delicate balance of slowing growth, persistent inflation and evolving political pressures. The U.S. Federal Reserve made a pivotal move in September, cutting its benchmark interest rate to a range of 4.00–4.25%. In Europe, the European Central Bank (ECB) maintained its aggressive easing trajectory, executing its eighth rate cut since mid-2024. The deposit rate now stands at 2.15%, as the ECB seeks to stimulate growth amid weak industrial output and subdued consumer confidence.

China benefited from targeted fiscal stimulus, accommodative monetary policy and strong performance in high-tech and consumer sectors which helped offset deflationary pressures and trade-related headwinds. Japan’s equity market rallied, buoyed by improving corporate governance, foreign investor inflows and a favorable trade agreement with the U.S.

Sector weights (%): fund vs. benchmark as of September 30, 2025

Quarterly portfolio recap

Both stock selection and sector allocation detracted from the fund’s relative returns compared to the benchmark for the quarter.

Positive contributions to the fund’s relative performance were led by financials, industrials and consumer staples sectors while the consumer discretionary, materials and communication services sectors detracted most significantly on a relative basis. In country terms, South Korea and Ireland led positive relative contributions while the United Kingdom and Switzerland detracted most from relative returns.

In the financials sector, Bank of Ireland (OTCPK:BKRIF)(OTCPK:BKRIY) and the Japanese financial services company, Orix (IX), both contributed positively to returns. Orix shares rose after the company reported strong quarterly results. These results and an announcement that the company is selling its stake in Indian renewables company, Greenko, both increased confidence in Orix’s ability to reach its return on equity (ROE) target. Bank of Ireland performed strongly on news of a favorable outcome in a Supreme Court judgement related to historic Motor Finance deals in the U.K.

Mitsubishi Electric (OTCPK:MIELF)(OTCPK:MIELY) was the largest contributor to returns in the industrials sector. The stock reacted positively to signs that they are beginning to execute on plans to improve their profitability, such as by exiting lower margin businesses and carrying out some workforce restructuring.

Lastly, in the consumer staples sector, Reckitt Benckiser (OTCPK:RBGPF) was a relative outperformer after they announced an agreement to sell their homecare business. This development made it possible for them to raise their sales growth guidance and sanction a buyback program.

Turning to the detractors, the largest negative in the consumer discretionary sector was the U.K. homebuilder Taylor Wimpey (OTCPK:TWODF)(OTCPK:TWODY), which saw earnings forecasts downgraded due to the weak U.K. housing backdrop.

The fund also suffered from not owning Alibaba (BABA), the Chinese e-commerce and cloud computing giant, which rose significantly over the quarter after the company’s results showed continued acceleration in their cloud business.

In the materials sector, the ingredients business DSM performed poorly on news that the planned sale of their animal health division was progressing more slowly than anticipated.

Lastly, in the communication services sector, Deutsche Telekom (OTCQX:DTEGY)(OTCQX:DLAKF) shares fell after weaker results from their German telecoms division raised concerns that competition in the industry may be increasing.

Outlook

The market remains swayed by ongoing uncertainty around tariffs, monetary policy and geopolitics along with the huge transition taking place in the world’s technology systems towards AI. On the one hand, the boom in data center capital expenditure (capex) and the associated growth in demand for power are driving strong growth in many areas of the semiconductor and industrial complexes. On the other hand, other key areas of economic activity remain challenged, such as the residential construction sector and lower income consumers. Against this backdrop, we maintain a balance in the portfolio between more cyclical companies that are positioned to benefit from those areas of the economy that are performing strongly and some more defensively positioned businesses.

Across all regions and sectors, we continue to look for high and growing free cash flow, sustainable profit margins, intelligent capital allocation and strong balance sheets. The focus on these quality factors has benefited the relative performance of the portfolio in times of stress and over full market cycles.

data.title: Gamco Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust is an income fund. The Fund intends to generate current income from short-term gains primarily through its strategy of writing (selling) covered call options on the equity securities in its portfolio. Because of its primary strategy, the Fund forgoes the opportunity to participate fully in the appreciation of the underlying equity security above the exercise price of the option. It also is subject to the risk of depreciation of the underlying equity security in excess of the premium received.

Investment Objective

The GAMCO Natural Resources, Gold & Income Trust is a non-diversified, closed-end management investment company. The Fund’s investment objective is to provide a high level of current income. The Fund’s secondary investment objective is to seek capital appreciation consistent with the Fund’s strategy and primary objective. Under normal market conditions, the Fund will attempt to achieve its objectives by investing 80% of its assets in equity securities of companies principally engaged in natural resource and gold industries, and by writing covered call options on the underlying equity securities.

Investment Discussion

Gold's exceptional momentum continues after a spectacular first half of 2025 in which gold surged 25.9%, the third quarter bringing no respite for bearish investors. Instead of the anticipated consolidation, the precious metal rally gained speed, fueled by the escalating U.S. fiscal crisis and furthered powered by the passage of the Big Beautiful Bill. Gold bullion rose an additional 16.8% in the third quarter, driving its year-to-date increase to a staggering 47.1%. The factors driving this flight to gold and the parallel trend of de-dollarization are numerous and structural: high U.S. debt-to-GDP ratio, high interest rates on that debt, a large budget deficit, geopolitical volatility, tariff volatility, and the "weaponization" of the dollar. As the global financial system searches for an alternative, gold remains the "shining angel" in the absence of many credible fiat alternatives. A further reduction in interest rates could amplify its appeal even more. Investor interest was strong, with private investors adding over 6 million ounces to gold-backed exchange-traded funds (ETFs) during the quarter. Total gold holdings across all ETFs reached 96.7 million ounces at the end of September, up from 82.9 million ounces at the start of the year. However, this is still well below the 2020 high of 111 million ounces, suggesting the rally may have room to run. Mirroring the commodity's performance, the Philadelphia Gold and Silver Index (XAU), a key benchmark for mining stocks, returned 44.9% for the quarter, reflecting a fair valuation of the underlying gold price increase.

In contrast to gold, the oil sector continues to struggle with oversupply. Pricing for West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude fluctuated, driven primarily by geopolitical dynamics rather than underlying demand, ultimately dropping 4.2% for the quarter. Despite the price drop, the market saw refining margins continue their downward trend, averaging around $25 per barrel. U.S. production remained steady at 13.5 million barrels per day, and OPEC production saw an expected increase, hitting 29 million barrels per day. While the commodity itself underperformed, energy equities, as represented by the Energy Select Sector Index (IXE), expanded by 6.3%. This gain suggests the equity market is performing more in line with the general market and discounting some of the near-term commodity price weakness.

During the third quarter of 2025, the agricultural sector, represented by the MVIS Global Agribusiness Index, was flat, while the general market was up 8.1%. The performance was driven mostly by Elanco Corp. (ELAN) +41.0%, Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM) +14.1%, Deere (DE) -9.8%, and Bayer (BAYN) +10.3%.

Volatility levels at the end of the third quarter increased in all sectors, with the gold sector at 38%, 32% for the base metals sector, 32% for agriculture, and 26% for energy equities. We have maintained a part of the gold equity section of the fund unhedged during the quarter. The maturity of the option portfolio was an average of 2.5 months. At the end of the quarter, the Fund’s participation across sectors was 55% for gold and mining, 48% for agriculture, and 50% for energy.

data.title: Under Armour: Turnaround Still Unproven
data.description:
Under Armour, Inc. (UAA) (UA) released its Q2 2026 Earnings earlier this month, and revenue has declined for the tenth consecutive quarter. Over the last year, the stock has fallen by 55.05%, and 74.25% over the last five years. The market sentiment has been negative due to declining revenue and negative earnings. Under Armour has tried a number of turnaround plans, but never quite got back to the boom and double-digit growth it experienced in the 2010s. At the moment, it is on a back-to-basics strategy, and while there are minor improvements, one thing that is clear is that the turnaround is far from complete. The company is not broken, but without clear growth drivers and a downward trend within its largest market, North America, the outlook looks challenging. My recommendation is a wait-and-see hold rating.

Company overview

Under Armour is a sports apparel and footwear company founded in 1996, which sells through its own retail and e-commerce channels as well as via wholesale partners such as sporting goods stores and department stores. What is important for investors is to know what the company no longer is. It has fully exited the fitness technology business after some costly but unsuccessful acquisitions. In 2020, it sold MyFitnessPal for less than the purchase price, and it shut down Endomondo. In 2024, it sold its MapMyFitness apps, which benefited cash flow in FY 2025. The company is an international company that generates sales across North America, Europe, Asia, and Latin America. Its most recent strategy for FY2025 is to focus on premium storytelling and brand focus. It is cutting down on unprofitable product lines, reducing discounts, and cutting down on inventory. It is clear that since demand started slowing down in 2017, the company has yet to find its footing in a very competitive sporting industry. The company has also seen one of its most significant partnerships end with Steph Curry, which could be seen as a concern, as only two years ago, the contract was extended to a "lifetime contract".

Under Armour is still a much smaller player than its larger peers in the market, like NIKE (NKE) and adidas (OTCQX:ADDYY). For context, I have compared Under Armour's revenue to the five largest sporting goods company peers in terms of market cap, as seen in the table below.

In FY 2025, revenue was $5.05 billion compared to Nike's annual sales of $46.44 billion. According to CSI Market data, we can see that Under Armour has only 2.36% of the industry's market share. Against these giants, we can see that Under Armour remains a niche player, although its brand is well known internationally. Investors should be aware that it faces fierce competition for athletic consumers and shelf space.

Growth drivers

Growth has been an issue for the company. Management has switched from portfolio diversification and is now back to emphasizing higher quality products and a simpler portfolio with a focus on its brand strength. One way in which this has been done is by reorganising offerings around key categories. During the Q2 2026 earnings call, management said that some of these actions are showing good early results, but at the same time, FY 2026 top line is expected to drop by up to 5% YoY. The company is also focused on cutting down on costs and emphasising that effort is focused on profitable segments. The company has been reducing inventory and paying down some of its short term debt. In Q2 2026, it eliminated a $600 million bond issue coming due in 2026. The worry for investors, though, is that sales have been declining in North America, its largest market, and within footwear.

Financial overview

Until FY2017, Under Armour's top line was growing at a double-digit rate, but it has never quite returned to this sort of growth, and more recently, we are seeing a decline YoY. TTM is $5.04 billion. In the FH 2026, we can see that the revenue has dropped YoY. In Q2 2026, sales dropped 5% YoY for the quarter. North America is the company's largest market, but it remains weak, dropping in sales by 8% YoY, while international sales were flat or declining slightly. The company's gross profit has seen a similar decline. Since FY2021, we can see a negative trend in the results. The company has tried to improve this by lowering supply chain costs and offering fewer discounts, but we have yet to see improvements.

Looking at net income makes me even more worried. In FY2025, the company had a net loss of $201.3 million, and we have seen a decline in financials since FY2023. The company expects its FY 2026 revenue to be down by another 4 to 5% and only slightly profitable.

Levered free cash flow has been inconsistent over the last five years, with strong positive years but also periods of outflows when Under Armour cut inventory, restructured operations, and dealt with weaker demand.

Under Armour's balance sheet is stable but not particularly strong. The company holds about $395.99 million in cash, which helps liquidity, but total debt is still high at $1.9 billion, which pushes debt-to-equity above 100%. The current ratio of 1.69 suggests they can cover near-term obligations, though the quick ratio of 0.62 shows that once inventory is removed, short-term liquidity is much tighter.

Valuation

When comparing Under Armour to major peers like NIKE, adidas (OTCQX:ADDYY), lululemon (LULU), Asics (AS), and ANTA (OTCPK:ANPDY), the stock looks cheap, but for good reasons. We already know that the stock value has dropped by over 50% in the last year. This indicates negative market sentiment and little faith in future results. Under Armour trades at a Price-to-Sales ratio of 0.36, far below its peers. Even on an enterprise value basis, the EV/Sales of 0.66 is a fraction of the multiples of its stronger global brands. It's priced more like a struggling brand than a growth story. Because Under Armour currently has negative earnings, its GAAP and non-GAAP P/E ratios aren't meaningful. The Price-to-Book ratio of 0.98 shows that the stock is trading almost exactly at the value of its net assets. This is something you typically see in companies that are restructuring, where the market isn't confident about future returns.

Risks

There are a number of issues investors should consider before investing in Under Armour. The company has really struggled to grow its market share. Its North American business is in decline, and the international markets are not doing much better. If it doesn't have success with its new product strategy, sales could continue to decline, creating serious problems for the overall financials of the company. Gaining market share is anything but easy. The competition is fierce, and there are giants such as Nike and Adidas that dominate through much larger budgets and retail potential. As a discretionary apparel company, it is sensitive to consumer spending trends. Any recession or weaker consumer spending could negatively impact an already declining business. While the company has a multi-year turnaround plan, any missteps could delay results. Investors need to believe in futures and management's word rather than the results at the moment.

Final thoughts

Under Armour may be an interesting company if you have bought into the management's turnaround story. It is focusing on the brand, cutting costs, and restructuring plan to show confidence in the business. However, the recent financial trends are worrying, and the Q2 2026 earnings results add to the concern rather than show any meaningful improvements. For now, with sales still in decline and negative annual earnings, although the stock is not expensive relative to its peers, I recommend a wait-and-see hold for long-term investors. If growth really returns, there could be value, but I would wait for a few quarters of improvement before that point.

data.title: IBM Unplugged: AI Buzz, Z Rebounds, And A Measured Outlook
data.description:
My Hold call on International Business Machines Corporation (IBM) in April is an opportunity lost on many counts. One, the market itself bounced back by ~27% (S&P 500). Most stock Buy recommendations made in April have benefited from the unidirectional surge of the past few months until the November corrections started. Secondly, I did find the thesis a strong defensive Hold, but did not graduate to a more enterprising "add fresh capital" view—seemingly an easier call for a company with IBM's defensive qualities.

Looking back, some of the thesis still holds true. More so today after a market-matching ~27% rally from the time of my analysis. I had talked up IBM's lack of growth which still remains, though the outlook on the AI and quantum optionalities supports a bullish narrative that it has ridden to its $300 journey. I had written in my thesis that:

Overall, I am comfortable with IBM’s valuation given its growth prospects even at these levels. Also, comfortable that it is way lower than big tech. IBM still makes sense to me in an overall IT portfolio, not for valuation expansion, but providing a dividend-paying safer defensive cushion while I leverage SaaS and big tech.

Of course, that was the correct stand, relatively speaking. IBM's low beta of 0.65 has meant it has not worked out as the best use of capital for buying in April. Any other growth-focused tech mega-cap has generally outdone the S&P 500 gains and several of my Buy calls have profited from that outlook. But IBM's standalone rating should still have been a Buy, instead of a relative Hold for its defensive qualities.

I had asked for more execution backing the AI promise and that has actually happened. In fact, IBM has executed strongly across AI, hybrid cloud, and IBM Z infrastructure, delivering steady revenue, margins and free cash flow. But the stock has also rallied significantly from the April levels. The biggest question facing an IBM thesis now is what has actually transpired that is beyond what is already priced into this rally. My take is that recent developments (accelerated AI adoption, incremental revenue from Watsonx agents, outperformance in hybrid cloud and Z) provide modest incremental upside from here—i.e., most of the upside is already captured. So, I won't chase IBM, particularly because chasing missed opportunities often leads to irrational decision-making. IBM remains a Hold—again, fairly priced and defensive with a strong dividend cushion within tech. It remains an integral part of any tech portfolio, but no fresh capital deployment is indicated looking at the risk-reward profile today.

What Has Changed

Since I had only the Q4 2024 numbers as the latest to look at, I have compared the numbers from Q4 2024 against the latest Q3 2025 data. Core software growth is largely the same at just about double digits (slightly down from Q4 2024 actually). Red Hat continues to grow, but again at slightly slower rates (from 16-17% to ~14% now). Consulting revenues have stabilized (from -2% growth to positive 2% growth now). Infrastructure support remains flat or marginally positive. These areas are steady contributors to topline and EBITDA, but since trends haven’t meaningfully accelerated, they don’t materially change forward EBITDA or revenue growth expectations. Transaction Processing has declined sharply (~10% to ~-2%).

Automation has made visible progress, surging from ~15% growth to ~24% growth now, potentially with decent margin leverage implications. Data and AI have also accelerated, but not as sharply—up from 4-5% growth in Q4 2024 to 8% growth today. Hybrid infrastructure has rebounded dramatically. IBM Z has turned around from a -20% growth to 61% in Q3 2025.

So it is not surprising to see a narrative build up on the hot themes of automation, AI, and IBM Z, and a lot of that is valid optimism (even if the quantum optionality is ignored in the short term). I expect the automation and AI growth trends to be margin accretive, but the topline growth in software overall has not budged by much—in fact, it has slowed marginally around double-digit growth levels.

The real growth turnaround has actually been around infrastructure (led by IBM Z) and consulting, narratives that are more cyclical and lumpy than structural. IBM Z’s rebound reflects the tail end of a mainframe refresh cycle—strong in this quarter but unlikely to sustain such outsized growth indefinitely. Consulting is stabilizing after prior weakness, driven by specific client project wins and digital transformation spend, and can fluctuate with macro changes.

In contrast, the hot themes of automation and AI are more structural, reflecting ongoing enterprise digitization and early AI adoption, but their absolute contribution to the overall software topline is still modest. Looking at the topline growth in software overall, AI should be a small base today. The AI growth rate has not been nearly as stellar as IBM Z's story. Also, the AI book of business has grown from $5b in Q4 2024 to $9.5b in Q3 2025—material, but not growing at the same rate as the $2b QoQ uptick it showed in Q4 2024. Additionally, consulting generative AI book of business signings declined in Q3.

Bird's-Eye View

Looking at the overall impact of all the AI growth and other turnaround narratives on the company's overall financial metrics provides more context on the levels of growth and earnings transformation underway in reality. The over-5% guidance on revenue growth for the full year 2025 translates to ~3% YoY growth in Q4. That is a slowdown from the near double-digit growth seen in the past couple of quarters. Even if I ignore the projected revenue growth of ~3% for Q4 (given the seasonally large quarter in revenue terms where guidance could be both conservative or suffer from tougher comps), the full-year ~5-6% growth level is not exactly what I can call motoring along.

EBITDA growth has been positive, though. In Q3 2025, IBM reported a ~21% growth in EBITDA YoY. A year ago, in Q3 2024, it had grown by ~7-8%. In Q2 2025, the EBITDA growth was ~15%, so the improvement trend is real. Even the TTM EBITDA trajectory looks healthy.

Since my April analysis (with Q4 2024 numbers as the latest numbers to go by), valuations have surged by 20-25% in EV to forward EBITDA terms (expanded from ~14.5x to ~17.5x).

Based on the latest quarter's EBITDA growth rate YoY, a 21% EBITDA growth profile combined with a ~25% valuation growth in EV/EBITDA shows how closely the share prices may have reacted to the EBITDA improvements. However, much of this EBITDA improvement may reflect non-structural factors, including the IBM Z mainframe cycle, rather than a durable acceleration of the core business. Revenue growth remains modest, and cash flow conversion has not improved materially (FCF margins flat at 20%). Also, the EBITDA used to calculate the uplift is based on adjusted figures that exclude recurring costs, making the assumption somewhat bullish.

Again, Fairly Valued At Best

Overall, the IBM thesis reached where it was in April—fairly valued. The AI uplift is promising. The quantum play is a good optionality. The best-case scenario in terms of EBITDA improvements seems to be factored in. Further improvements remain heavily dependent on continued execution.

The AI business primarily focuses on enterprise AI solutions, integrating AI into software, consulting, and infrastructure to drive incremental growth and margin improvement. It also faces competition from both hyperscalers and specialized enterprise AI vendors. The AI impact is unlikely to show up in the next couple of quarters visibly in terms of numbers and will not be dramatically transformative, unlike what the buzzword association suggests.

IBM's dividend yield and defensive characteristics continue to make it a great stock to hold, but I don't want to allocate fresh capital, having missed the April to October rally anyway. In fact, I will await a 10-20% pullback to offer a decent safety buffer—critical when playing defensive tech.

data.title: 'Tis The Season For A Broad EM Core - And A Dash Of Dynamic Tilts
data.description:
By Dina Ting, CFA

It’s that time of year again. Holiday shoppers know the secret: start with a meaningful primary gift that makes an impression. Add smaller delights for a personal touch. Asset allocators can do the same - anchor portfolios with a broad emerging market (EM) core and use dynamic tilts1 for the perfect stocking stuffers.

The broad EM equity rally has now entered a more structurally supportive phase rather than a pure sentiment bounce. EM equities have advanced for 10 straight months, now up more than 32.3% year to date, outpacing US large caps, which returned slightly more than 14.6% over the same period.2

We believe this outperformance is likely to continue through year‑end amid a weaker US dollar, improving earnings and growing demand for geographic diversification.

Valuation gaps remain wide: EM equities recently traded at nearly a 40% discount versus US peers - one of their lowest forward price-to-earnings (P/E) differentials in over a decade. Meanwhile, early macro indicators suggest modest expansion among EM manufacturing sectors.

EM vs. US Forward P/E Ratios (10-Year Range)

December 30, 2014–October 31, 2025

Sources: FactSet, MSCI, FactSet Market Aggregates. Next 12 months (NTM) P/E: MSCI EM Index Premium/Discount over MSCI USA Index. The MSCI EM Index is a free float-weighted equity index. The MSCI USA Index is designed to broadly and fairly represent the full diversity of business activities in the United States. Indexes are unmanaged and one cannot directly invest in them. They do not include fees, expenses or sales charges. Past performance is not an indicator or a guarantee of future performance. See www.franklintempletondatasources.com for additional data provider information.

Adding to this tailwind, the recent decline in the US dollar is easing financial conditions across EMs. The weaker greenback makes it cheaper for EM borrowers to service dollar-denominated debt, while the Federal Reserve’s pivot toward interest-rate cuts is fueling renewed demand for local-currency bonds that still offer attractive real yields.

Meanwhile, deepening trade and manufacturing linkages between the United States and Mexico underscore how supply chain rerouting is boosting multiple EM hubs - not just one market - reinforcing the case for a broad EM core. These forces of less dollar pressure, falling US rates and stronger regional trade flows are creating what we see as a more favorable backdrop for EMs.

In terms of portfolio construction, a diversified EM allocation anchors exposure to global easing, demographic growth and digital transformation, while selective country tilts reflect conviction-driven opportunities.

Such an approach helps investors look beyond short-term noise and stay invested through the macro cycle. With valuations still moderate, we believe the risk-reward for EMs broadly remains compelling.

Why broad core + dynamic tilts work now

Global supply chain remapping triggered by tariffs has created more stark standouts and laggards across the EM universe and we believe a broad EM core can help capture the multiplicity of growth vectors, while dynamic tilts allow investors to capture standout growth pockets when dispersion widens. South Korea’s equity market, for example, has emerged as a clear leader this year, up nearly 70% year-to-date - the strongest returns for any major market globally.3

This surge has been powered by a combination of AI-driven demand for memory chips, foreign-investor inflows returning after years of under-allocation, and corporate-governance reforms that are helping erase the long-standing “Korea discount.”4

Country Contribution to EM Index Returns (Rolling 12 Months)

2019–2025 YTD

Source: Bloomberg, as of October 31, 2025. Bloomberg Emerging Markets Large & Mid Cap Index, Total Return, USD. The Bloomberg Emerging Markets Large & Mid Cap Total Return Index is a float market-cap-weighted equity benchmark that covers the top 85% of market cap of the measured market. Indexes are unmanaged and one cannot directly invest in them. They do not include fees, expenses or sales charges. Past performance is not an indicator or a guarantee of future performance. See www.franklintempletondatasources.com for additional data provider information.

By contrast, China’s contribution has moderated, reflecting slower post-COVID 19 normalization and softer capital inflows. Based on estimates derived from the Brookings/Haver Analytics dataset covering 25 EMs, China’s share of total EM portfolio inflows appears to have fallen - from roughly 40%–50% before the pandemic to below 20% by mid-2025.5 This indicates a reallocation of capital toward faster-growing, reform-oriented economies such as India, Mexico and Brazil.

Nonetheless, we believe Beijing’s support of the country’s real estate sector and injections of liquidity into its equity markets have been notable. Its leaders have adopted a more measured, targeted stance in supporting businesses and consumers, while gradually rebuilding investor confidence.

Additionally, the contribution to returns from China - which holds the largest weighting at 32% vs. 10% for South Korea - within emerging market indexes has turned positive, adding 9.3% year-to-date through October 31, 2025.6 This suggests that improving earnings sentiment and valuation support are beginning to reassert China’s role within the broader EM complex.

In markets, as with the holidays, balance matters: a broad EM core potentially provides staying power, while thoughtful tilts can deliver the finishing touch. Together, we believe this approach may help investors turn dispersion into opportunity for the year ahead.

Endnotes

  1. Dynamic tilt is an investment strategy that systematically adjusts asset allocation to favor factors or assets that are expected to perform well based on current market conditions, rather than based on a static, long-term allocation.
  2. Source: Bloomberg, as of November 14, 2025. EM equities measured by the FTSE Emerging Index; US large caps measured by the S&P 500 Index. The FTSE Emerging Index provides investors with a comprehensive means of measuring the performance of the most liquid large- and mid-cap companies in the EMs. Indexes are unmanaged and one cannot directly invest in them. They do not include fees, expenses or sales charges. Past performance is not an indicator or a guarantee of future performance. See www.franklintempletondatasources.com for additional data provider information.
  3. Source: Bloomberg, as of November 9, 2025. The Korean Composite Stock Price Index (KOSPI) is a series of indexes that track the overall Korean Stock Exchange and its components. Indexes are unmanaged and one cannot directly invest in them. They do not include fees, expenses or sales charges. Past performance is not an indicator or a guarantee of future performance. See www.franklintempletondatasources.com for additional data provider information.
  4. The Korea discount is the phenomenon where South Korean companies are valued lower than their global peers due to various factors like poor corporate governance, weak shareholder returns and geopolitical risks.
  5. Source: “Trends in global capital flows to emerging markets.” Brookings Institution. November 5, 2025.
  6. Source: Bloomberg. The Bloomberg EM Large & Mid Cap Total Return Index is a float market-cap-weighted equity benchmark that covers the top 85% of market cap of the measured market. data.title: Barings BDC: A Rare Moment When Common Stock Beat Bonds
data.description:
We continue our bottom-picking effort in the BDC sector with another pick today. Readers are used to our baby bond articles in the sector, but many of these trades are currently without Alpha, so we have to look at the discounted equity in the sector. The following lines are about a closed-end management investment company that has elected to be regulated as a BDC: Barings BDC Inc. (BBDC).

Company Basic Info

  • Total Assets (Q3): approximately $2.82 billion
  • Current Market Capitalization: close to $932 million
  • Portfolio:

  • Historical Performance:

The 3-year total return on NAV is around 9.31%, and the 5-year total return is close to 8.94%.

  • 10-year premium/discount chart:

  • Credit Rating:

Fitch: BBB-/Moody's: Baa3

  • Bond Yields:

BBDC's over-the-counter bonds have Baa3 credit ratings. The yield spread of its bonds at the time of writing is approximately 2.18% compared to the Treasury yield curve, which is among the lowest in the sector.

Historical Performance

For that part, we have chosen several basic metrics that are most important to us for determining the risk profile and future return capability.

  • Assets yield stripped of non-leverage expenses and realized and unrealized profits through the years. This gives us the yield after management fees and management performance if the entity were not to be leveraged. The average asset yield for the past 10 years is around 3.32%.

  • Non-leverage Expenses

Non-leverage expenses average 2.05% of total assets (noting that this is a simplified average and does not reflect expense structure details):

  • ROE

With almost 10 years of data, we can make a fairly reliable forecast for the future of this indicator. The average return on equity is around 2.9%.

Losses as a percentage of total income

BBDC loses, on average, about 30% of its investing income. This is mainly due to the company's large losses in 2017 and 2018. The same reason for the average ROE:

We have provided 10 years of data; however, the company suffered significant losses in 2017-2018 under its former name, Triangle Capital Corporation (now Barings BDC), due to poor investment performance in 2017 and the subsequent sale of the entire portfolio below book value in 2018 to Benefit Street Partners (for approximately $981 million). For these reasons, the projections that follow will be based on data from the past three years.

Projections and Determining a Fair Value Discount for BBDC

To simplify the model, we use several assumptions, as future yields and the yield curve are uncertain. We will assume an average SOFR for the next 5 years and adjust the asset return accordingly. Leverage costs are calculated based on historical spreads and debt structure. Operating expenses are around 2%. For portfolio losses, we use a historical average of 3% of total income. All the averages are for the last 3 years.

Below, we can see what the expected ROE is based on how poorly we expect the portfolio to perform. For example, if we assume that 30% of total income will be lost in the future, the expected ROE would be approximately 2.88%:

The common stock of the company is currently trading at $8.87. The average discount for the last 5 years is 15.88%, above the current discount of 20.09%.

The projected NII yield is close to 10.04%, and the expected adjusted ROE at a 20% discount, based on our averages, is close to 10.76%.

Comparison with a leveraged bond of the company

When we leverage, the company's highest-yielding OTC bond carries a lower ROE than the stock, close to 8.10%, even using the 5-year swap rate as a leverage cost.

Conclusion

While BDC baby bonds remain the safer bet with a projected yield of around 9% when leveraged, BBDC's heavily discounted common stock currently offers higher return potential - close to 11% according to our models. This comes with a higher risk, but for now, we prefer exposure to common stocks over leveraged OTC bonds, whose expected ROE remains lower.
